entries.title: State Platform Regulation: A Roadmap to Coming Debates
entries.subtitle: An analysis of state-led technology policy development across privacy, content moderation, antitrust, child safety, and taxation.
entries.publisher: NYU Center for Social Media, AI, and Politics
entries.abstract: This report examines the emerging role of U.S. states as primary drivers of technology policy development, particularly following Congressional gridlock on sector-wide governance reforms. Drawing on analysis of 28 platform regulation bills enacted over two years, the document maps state legislative activity across five key domains: privacy, content regulation, antitrust enforcement, child safety protections, and digital taxation. The report projects increased state-level technology policymaking in subsequent legislative sessions, driven by political alignment in trifecta governments and anticipated pressure surrounding reproductive rights and online speech regulation.

entries.title: State Technology Policy in 2023: An Overview of Legislative Developments
entries.subtitle: Analysis of 65 technology policy laws passed by U.S. states during 2023 legislative sessions and emerging state-level policy trends.
entries.publisher: NYU Center for Social Media, AI, and Politics
entries.abstract: This report examines technology policy developments across U.S. states during 2023, a year in which state legislatures took the lead in tech policy reform while federal efforts stalled on issues like privacy and online child safety. The authors document 65 technology policy laws passed by states and analyze changes in the political architecture of state-level technology governance. The report provides an overview of key legislation across six policy topics and relevant state litigation activity, representing the second installment in a series on state technology policy trends.

entries.title: State Technology Policy Developments: Third Annual Report
entries.subtitle: An overview of state-level technology policy changes, legislative developments, and litigation during 2024.
entries.publisher: NYU Center for Social Media, AI, and Politics
entries.abstract: This report provides a comprehensive analysis of developments in state technology policy during 2024, examining the evolving political architecture at the state legislative level. The authors review significant legislative and litigation developments in state tech policy, building on previous reports in this ongoing series. The document synthesizes key policy changes and legal developments affecting technology regulation across United States jurisdictions.

entries.title: The Center on Technology Policy: State Technology Policy Report 2025
entries.publisher: NYU Center for Social Media, AI, and Politics
entries.abstract: This report provides an overview of developments in state technology policy over the year of 2025. It analyzes the changing political architecture at the state level during the 2025 legislative sessions, reviews key developments in state technology legislation and litigation during that same year, and provides predictions on what the state policy landscape could look like in 2026.

entries.title: Ethical Analysis of Normothermic Regional Perfusion (NRP)
entries.abstract:
This paper outlines the ethical principles relevant for review of the new medical transplant procedure, normothermic regional perfusion (NRP). NRP is a new technology that has potential to increase the number of transplantable organs, but it is important to ensure that the donation procedures are transparent and ethical.

NRP involves using a machine to pass blood through organs in a personâs body after the heart has irreversibly stopped beating. Vessels are clamped during this procedure to prevent blood flow to the brain.

As a surgical procedure NRP shows a positive impact on utility by reducing non-use of organs without negatively impacting patient outcomes. However, the fact that NRP involves recirculation after declaration of death by circulatory criteria raises concerns about compliance with the Dead Donor Rule and nonmaleficence (do no harm). To uphold the ethical principle of respect for persons, the Ethics Committee considers that informed consent procedures should be thorough and include information about the unique and relevant aspects of NRP. Uncontrolled scenarios (as explained, in detail, in the white paper) for NRP raise serious concerns for respect for persons and hastily moving from therapeutic treatment to organ recovery.

entries.title: The FTC's Algorithmic Accountability Agenda: A Two-Year Review
entries.subtitle: A comprehensive review of FTC enforcement actions, policy statements, and rulemaking initiatives related to algorithmic systems from 2023 to 2025.
entries.abstract: This report surveys the Federal Trade Commission's enforcement and rulemaking activity on algorithmic systems over the 2023Ã¢ÂÂ2025 period. It catalogs enforcement actions, maps the policy statements and workshop outputs that defined the agency's agenda, and assesses which initiatives are likely to survive the current administration transition.

entries.title: Digital Markets Act at One Year: Lessons for U.S. Competition Policy
entries.subtitle: Evaluates the DMA's first year of enforcement and draws concrete lessons for U.S. policymakers considering analogous legislation.
entries.abstract: The EU's Digital Markets Act completed its first year of active enforcement in 2024. This report evaluates the Commission's first year of enforcement activity Ã¢ÂÂ gatekeeper designations, interoperability rulings, and compliance investigations Ã¢ÂÂ and draws structured lessons for U.S. legislators and antitrust enforcers considering comparable regulation.
entries.preview: The European Commission's assessment of the DMA's first year: gatekeeper designations, early enforcement patterns, and the compliance challenges that reveal where the regulation's design assumptions have held up and where they haven't.

entries.title: State AI Legislation Tracker: 2024 Year in Review
entries.subtitle: Surveys AI-related legislation introduced and passed across all 50 states in 2024, identifying trends, model bills, and areas of regulatory divergence.
entries.abstract: In 2024, more than 40 states introduced legislation touching on artificial intelligence. This report catalogs that legislation, maps the topical clusters Ã¢ÂÂ employment, consumer protection, public sector use, generative AI Ã¢ÂÂ and identifies the model bills most likely to shape legislative activity in 2025.

entries.title: Foundation Model Providers and the Regulatory Landscape: An Industry Overview
entries.subtitle: Maps the current regulatory exposure of major foundation model providers across jurisdictions.
entries.abstract: This report maps the current regulatory exposure of the major foundation model providers Ã¢ÂÂ including obligations under the EU AI Act, U.S. executive orders, and pending state legislation Ã¢ÂÂ and analyzes how the compliance landscape differs by provider size, deployment context, and jurisdiction.
entries.preview: RAND's mapping of the regulatory exposure facing major foundation model providers across the EU AI Act, U.S. executive orders, and state legislation Ã¢ÂÂ a useful compliance landscape survey for developers and deployers.
